title: Found-object collage page
subtitle: Building a scrapbook spread from collected ephemera
excerpt: A found-object collage page is built from printed matter, packaging, labels, tickets, pressed plants, and other paper ephemera collected over time. The design challenge is arranging these items so that they read as a coherent spread rather than a pile of scraps. This tutorial covers the planning sequence, the layering order, and the adhesives suited to different types of found material.
sourceNotes: Original tutorial based on collage and scrapbooking practices as established in the mixed-media craft community.

glossaryTerms.definition: Printed or handmade paper items originally produced for temporary use, such as tickets, receipts, envelopes, labels, postcards, and packaging. In scrapbooking and collage, ephemera is collected and used as decorative and narrative material.

The base surface onto which collage elements are adhered. In a journal or scrapbook, the page itself is the collage ground. Heavier paper (160 gsm or above) is more stable as a ground, especially when multiple layers of paper are applied.

The sequence in which collage elements are applied to the ground. Background layers (large, plain) go down first; foreground elements (small, detailed) go last. The layering order determines which elements appear to be in front.

content.text: A found-object collage begins with the
collection, not the design. Gather more material than you expect to use. Sort by colour, scale, and texture rather than by theme. The
are decided by dry-laying the elements before any adhesive is applied.

items.name: Found ephemera
items.notes: Tickets, labels, packaging paper, printed paper, envelopes, postcards, torn pages from old books or maps. Collect at least twice as much as you expect to use.

items.name: Scrapbook or journal with sturdy pages
items.notes: The collage adds weight and thickness; 200 gsm pages handle multiple layers better than 80 gsm pages.

items.name: Glue stick
items.notes: For adhering lightweight paper elements without wrinkling.

items.name: Double-sided tape
items.notes: For adhering heavier items (cardboard, thick labels) and for photo mounting.

items.name: Washi tape (optional)
items.notes: For borders, tab labels, and decorative banding.

content.text: Lay all the elements on the page without adhesive. Move them around until the arrangement feels balanced. Background elements (large, plain, or textured) should sit behind the focal items (smaller, more detailed, or most significant). Aim for variation in scale: mixing large and small elements creates more visual interest than elements of uniform size. Photograph or sketch the arrangement before lifting anything to adhere.

Apply adhesive and fix background layers first, working forward in the layering order to the foreground elements last. Use glue stick for thin paper, double-sided tape for heavier items. Press each element flat with the bone folder after adhering. Allow background layers to dry before placing elements on top of them.

Add handwriting, date labels, or caption text after all ephemera is adhered. Writing directly on ephemera is also effective. Close the journal and press under a weight for an hour: a collage spread with several layers of paper dries unevenly and can warp the pages if not pressed flat.
